title: Thoughts On Integers · Luna’s Blog
excerpt: In this post I’ll lay out my views on integer types, and how I’ve come to those views. Hopefully you’ll disagree initially and be convinced by the end!
content_html:
In this post I’ll lay out my views on integer types, and how I’ve come to those views. Hopefully you’ll disagree initially and be convinced by the end!

The status quo

Let’s begin with what I’d characterize as the status quo in popular programming languages today. But first, some caveats: I won’t mention scripting languages throughout this article for obvious reasons. Moreover, I’m leaving Java out of this section because it has some weirdness surrounding integers (int vs Integer and no distinct unsigned types).

We’ll start with a classic systems programming language, C. In addition to the traditional C integer types int, char, short, long, long long, etc, C99 added fixed size types with names like int8_t, uint64_t, and so on. On modern 64-bit platforms int is always signed and 32 bits wide. Additionally, C has a confusing array of machine-dependent types such as ptrdiff_t and size_t. In general, subscripts and sizes are of type size_t, but C’s implicit conversions mean you’ll often see int or something else used instead. When signed integers overflow in C, they invoke undefined behavior, meaning the compiler assumes the overflow never occurs. Overflow for unsigned integers, on the other hand, is defined to wrap.

Next up, C#, which I’d characterize as a typical “application programming” language that’s used when low level control isn’t necessary. C#’s types are named similarly to C’s: int, short, long and byte. int is 32 bits wide. Unsigned versions are obtained by prefixing the type with u. Subscripts and sizes use a plain int, and by default integers wrap on overflow.

Go is a contemporary alternative to languages in the same domain as C#. Here we see the beginning of a trend: rather than using unintuitive C-style integer type names, Go just appends the bitwidth of each type: int8, uint64, etc. Another interesting difference crops up in the size of int and uint: they match the size of an address on the target platform, rather than being hardcoded to a particular width. Again, sizes and subscripts use int. Go defines integer overflow to wrap.

Swift is yet another recent language in this “compiled statically-typed non-low-level commercial software” niche, and its integer types are similar to Go in several ways. Type names are identical to Go except for capitalization: Int8, UInt64, etc. Int and UInt have the same sizing behavior as Go, and subscripts & sizes both use Int. Curiously, Swift defaults to panicking on integer overflow, even in release builds.

Rust: a better approach

Did you notice a commonality in all the examples I listed above? All those languages have some kind of default int type. They require extra typing to use unsigned types instead of signed ones and explicitly-sized types instead of the default-sized int. I’d say this encourages the use of int over other types. After all: would you rather stare at a screenful of ints or uint32_ts? This goes beyond aesthetics, too. It’s so easy to take the lazy way out and type int without thinking, rather than scrutinize the use-case to identify the best type for the job.

Rust eliminates each of these points: all integer types have an explicit size, and all types are prefixed with either i or u for signed and unsigned respectively. There’s no bias towards a certain size or signedness. You’re forced to consider the circumstances and make a judgement on what type is most appropriate to the situation. Maybe memory is conserved as integers are narrowed, and bugs are prevented as – to invoke a dogma oft-cited by the Rust community – invalid values (negative numbers) become unrepresentable.

namesigned?size in bits
u8no8
u16no16
u32no32
u64no64
usizenoaddress-sized
i8yes8
i16yes16
i32yes32
i64yes64
isizeyesaddress-sized

One thing you might notice about this table is the presence of usize and isize. usize is used for subscripts and sizes, just like size_t in C. Contrary to C, Rust doesn’t have implicit integer casting, so you really do have to use usize for your array indexes (unless you want to drown in as-casts). Using usize rather than a 32-bit int means arrays can have more than two billion elements, and using an unsigned type means negative array indexes can never occur.

Rust panics on overflow in debug builds to catch bugs, but uses wrapping in release builds for performance. Note the lack of UB on overflow.

There is a caveat to what I said about Rust not being biased to a particular integer type:

fn demo() {
	let x = 1; // x: i32
}

Rust defaults to i32 when it has no type information for an integer literal, so there is an (albeit minor) bias to signed 32-bit integers. In my experience of Rust, though, I found I used signed integers so rarely that I’d almost advocate for making unsigned integers the default. If Rust didn’t put unsigned and signed integers on equal footing I’d likely have never reached this conclusion.

To me, all of these changes seem like obvious improvements.

A Rust programmer tries other languages

When I started experimenting with C#, Swift and Go I quickly became frustrated. With my attitude of picking the right integer type for the task, I attempted to use uint for array indexes everywhere instead of the signed int these languages default to. It was impractical due to the amount of casting required, so I gave up in frustration. This just made me more certain: why can’t every language have integer types like Rust?

Why does C make signed integer overflow UB, anyway?

Originally the reason was differences in hardware behavior, but today the argument you’ll hear most often is performance. But don’t most CPUs perform wrapping when an arithmetic operation overflows, anyway? Why not just define overflow to wrap (at no extra cost, since it comes with the CPU “for free”) and be done with it?

I watched a talk by Chandler Carruth on the topic of UB, in which he includes an enlightening example. (We’ll assume we’re running on an architecture with 64-bit addresses.1)

bool mainGtU(int32_t i1, int32_t i2, uint8_t *block)
{
	uint8_t c1, c2;

	c1 = block[i1]; c2 = block[i2];
	if (c1 != c2) return c1 > c2;
	i1++; i2++;

	c1 = block[i1]; c2 = block[i2];
	if (c1 != c2) return c1 > c2;
	i1++; i2++;

	// repeats several more times
}

The compiler is smart enough to eliminate the mutation of i1 and i2. Bytes are loaded directly from block + i1 and block + i2, then block + i1 + 1 and block + i2 + 1, then block + i1 + 2 and block + i2 + 2, and so on. These address calculations are done as part of the load instruction itself, so they occur within the space of a 64-bit integer.

Say we had forced the compiler to wrap on overflow. i1++; and i2++; will wrap when they reach the limit of a 32-bit integer. To ensure the runtime behavior is faithful to this wrapping, the compiler generates extra instructions which make block + i1 + n wrap back around to block + 0 when i1 + n overflows 32 bits. In other words, “the CPU does wrapping on overflow for free” doesn’t hold in this case, and we get bloated code as a result.

None of this is hypothetical; making i1 and i2 unsigned causes these extra instructions to be emitted, too. In his talk, Chandler uses this code as an example of how switching from unsigned to signed integers can improve performance because it lets the compiler exploit UB on overflow. He goes as far to say that

if you have an integer which you want to treat arithmetically as opposed to in some modular space (on some power of two), Make. It. Signed. If you need more bits, get more bits. Keep it signed.

This was fascinating to me. Aren’t you supposed to use the type system to prevent bugs? If a number can never be negative, why would you ever make it signed? Chandler’s perspective on this issue made me uncomfortable.

A foray further into C

As I immersed myself in systems programming using C, I came across several people who kept saying that signed integers should be used for indexes, even though they can’t be negative. In particular, I read three different posts on Chris Wellons’s blog which all support this view:

In recent years I’ve been convinced that unsigned sizes were a serious error, probably even one of the great early computing mistakes, and that sizes and subscripts should be signed. Not only that, pkg-config has no business dealing with gigantic objects! We’re talking about short strings and tiny files. If it ends up with a large object, then there’s a defect somewhere — either in itself or the system — and it should abort. Therefore sizes and subscripts are a natural int!

See that link in the quoted paragraph? It links to a short document written by none other than Bjarne Stroustrop, who also says that sizes and subscripts should be signed. With a skeptical attitude and my Rust-based preconceptions I didn’t find his arguments very convincing. Still, there was a niggling feeling in the back of my mind that all these people must have a point.

Looking at some C alternatives

Odin is one of a fray of up-and-coming C alternatives which focuses on simplicity and “the joy of programming”. After some failed experimentation with another C alternative, Zig, I decided to take a closer look at Odin.

Once again, the familiar “modern language” integer system appears, with a few twists oriented towards low-level programming:

  • int and uint are address-sized
  • i8 through to i128 are signed
  • u8 through to u128 are unsigned
  • explicitly-sized types can be suffixed with either le or be to make their in-memory representation little-endian or big-endian
  • sizes and subscripts use int
  • wrap on overflow

This time, I decided to have an open mind and take a closer look at what benefits this setup might have. Some of the arguments below aren’t mine, but I can’t remember which ones I picked up from where and which ones are my own.

Address-sized is the perfect default size

As we saw earlier, mixing pointers and 32-bit integers can lead to performance problems if we aren’t willing to make overflow UB. Had we made i1 and i2 be of type ptrdiff_t or something similar, then wrapping on overflow would’ve worked just fine. Moreover, ISAs such as A64 only have 32- and 64-bit arithmetic instructions, so operating on 8- and 16-bit integers requires an extra and instruction. Performing all arithmetic on address-sized integers is generally the best option.

One counterargument to this is that modern CPUs are usually memory-bound, so packing data structures to be as small as possible is a good idea. Making integers address-sized rather than defaulting to, say, 32 bits, goes against this goal. Regardless, I’d say the simplicity of being able to use a plain int for array indexes, sizes, arithmetic, and as a default integer type outweigh the cost of having to remember to use int32 where appropriate.

In release builds, wrapping on overflow is a fine tradeoff

Making overflow undefined behavior has no benefit2 if all integers being operated on are address-sized. I’d rather have a couple casts at the top of a function than more UB time bombs waiting to explode.

Given the roughly 30% overhead of overflow checks, I think restricting them to debug builds is a reasonable tradeoff. I can also see an argument for using wrap on overflow even in debug builds for consistency and simplicity.

Unsigned integers are unintuitive

Let’s say you want to loop from a number down to zero. It’s trivial with signed integers:

void demo(int32_t top)
{
	for (int32_t i = top; i >= 0; i--) {
		printf("%d\n", i);
	}
}

As you’d expect, running demo(3) prints

3
2
1
0

i and top are never negative here, though, so surely unsigned integers are better in these circumstances?

void demo(uint32_t top)
{
	for (uint32_t i = top; i >= 0; i--) {
		printf("%u\n", i);
	}
}

This is an endless loop. For an unsigned integer n, n >= 0 is always true. Since unsigned integers wrap on overflow, when i reaches zero we decrement it and reach 4,294,967,295. To fix this we can change the condition to i <= top:

void demo(uint32_t top)
{
	for (uint32_t i = top; i <= top; i--) {
		printf("%u\n", i);
	}
}

Once i has wrapped around, it will be greater than top and as such break out of the loop. Just awful.

Won’t signed integers make bounds checks slower?

Performing a bounds check when using signed indexes requires checking both 0 <= i and i < count. These two checks are independent from one another, and so a contemporary CPU can run them in parallel. Rarely are all the ALUs of a CPU saturated, so removing the first of these checks likely won’t change how long the bounds check takes to execute.

Unsigned integers are more bug-prone

Suppose we have two array indexes, i and j. Suppose also that the overhead of panicking on overflow is unacceptable for our use-case, so we’re wrapping on overflow. We wish to find how many elements lie between i and j, and want to copy them to a new array. Simple, right? j - i is all that’s needed to determine the number of elements, and then we can create a new array of that size and perform the copy.

Imagine that j is always greater than or equal to i in our test data, but somehow ends up less than i in production. If i and j are unsigned, j - i will wrap around to what is probably a very large number. We end up creating an array of that very large size; if we’re lucky the copy has bounds checks and causes a panic.

The point is that an unsigned underflow is hard to catch: there’s no way to tell whether a large number was erroneously caused by wrapping or if its presence is intentional. Using signed integers here means the invalid j - i subtraction produces a “poisoned” (negative) result immediately. This result can be caught straight away when it’s used to create an array, rather than later on through the unpredictable flow-on effects of huge numbers.

Won’t signed integers require more asserts?

It is true that to have caught that negative array size our array allocation code would’ve needed an assert(count > 0). However, to have caught it if we had been using unsigned integers requires much more than just an assert – we’d need to enable comprehensive overflow checking for the entire program.

You can view the asserts you need to add when using signed integers as a lightweight form of overflow checking. Rather than testing for overflow after every single arithmetic operation, we can just put an equivalent check at the top of every function. In other words, a large number of overflow checks have been coalesced into a single check at an API boundary.

Now, of course a single assert(count > 0) isn’t equivalent to thoroughly checking for overflows everywhere. For instance, it wouldn’t catch something like create_array(1000 + j - i). There’s a tradeoff between performance and safety here, and I’d say that signed integers with liberal usage of asserts and wrapping on overflow is a happy middle-ground.

Don’t signed integers have a lower maximum value, so you’ll need to use more bits?

I’d disagree with this. Given we’re on a 64-bit platform, being limited to 63 bits’ worth of elements in an array really doesn’t matter, and if anything is probably a good thing. For instance, Rust limits the size of all allocations to the maximum of a signed address-sized integer so APIs like ptr::offset(isize) can function.

For smaller bit-widths, like if we’re using 32-bit indexes into an array for compactness, losing one bit won’t make much of a difference anyway. I don’t know of any applications where a maximum of two billion elements isn’t sufficient, but four billion is.

  1. I am aware that most “64-bit” CPUs actually have 48- or 52-bit addresses. Let’s just go with “64-bit addresses” for simplicity. ↩︎

  2. Overflow checks are an exception. Making overflow UB means the compiler can eliminate overflow checks, potentially improving performance. However, I’d argue that this is yet another case of C prioritizing performance over correctness, and that those checks shouldn’t be removed in the first place. ↩︎

Luna Razzaghipour
14 September 2023

blocks.html_en: Let’s begin with what I’d characterize as the status quo in popular programming languages today. But first, some caveats: I won’t mention scripting languages throughout this article for obvious reasons. Moreover, I’m leaving Java out of this section because it has some weirdness surrounding integers (int vs Integer and no distinct unsigned types).

sentences.en: Let’s begin with what I’d characterize as the status quo in popular programming languages today.
sentences.zh: 让我们从我所描述的当今流行编程语言的现状开始。

sentences.en: But first, some caveats: I won’t mention scripting languages throughout this article for obvious reasons.
sentences.zh: 但首先，有一些警告：出于显而易见的原因，我不会在本文中提及脚本语言。

sentences.en: Moreover, I’m leaving Java out of this section because it has some weirdness surrounding integers (int vs Integer and no distinct unsigned types).
sentences.zh: 此外，我将 Java 排除在本节之外，因为它在整数方面有一些奇怪的地方（int 与 Integer，并且没有明显的无符号类型）。

blocks.html_en: We’ll start with a classic systems programming language, C. In addition to the traditional C integer types int, char, short, long, long long, etc, C99 added fixed size types with names like int8_t, uint64_t, and so on. On modern 64-bit platforms int is always signed and 32 bits wide. Additionally, C has a confusing array of machine-dependent types such as ptrdiff_t and size_t. In general, subscripts and sizes are of type size_t, but C’s implicit conversions mean you’ll often see int or something else used instead. When signed integers overflow in C, they invoke undefined behavior, meaning the compiler assumes the overflow never occurs. Overflow for unsigned integers, on the other hand, is defined to wrap.

sentences.en: We’ll start with a classic systems programming language, C.
sentences.zh: 我们将从经典的系统编程语言 C 开始。

sentences.en: In addition to the traditional C integer types int, char, short, long, long long, etc, C99 added fixed size types with names like int8_t, uint64_t, and so on.
sentences.zh: 除了传统的 C 整数类型 int、char、short、long、long long 等之外，C99 还添加了固定大小类型，名称为 int8_t、uint64_t 等。

sentences.en: On modern 64-bit platforms int is always signed and 32 bits wide.
sentences.zh: 在现代 64 位平台上，int 始终是有符号的且宽度为 32 位。

sentences.en: Additionally, C has a confusing array of machine-dependent types such as ptrdiff_t and size_t.
sentences.zh: 此外，C 还有一组令人困惑的机器相关类型，例如 ptrdiff_t 和 size_t。

sentences.en: In general, subscripts and sizes are of type size_t, but C’s implicit conversions mean you’ll often see int or something else used instead.
sentences.zh: 一般来说，下标和大小的类型为 size_t，但 C 的隐式转换意味着您经常会看到使用 int 或其他类型。

sentences.en: When signed integers overflow in C, they invoke undefined behavior, meaning the compiler assumes the overflow never occurs.
sentences.zh: 当有符号整数在 C 中溢出时，它们会调用未定义的行为，这意味着编译器假设溢出永远不会发生。

sentences.en: Overflow for unsigned integers, on the other hand, is defined to wrap.
sentences.zh: 另一方面，无符号整数的溢出被定义为换行。

blocks.html_en: Next up, C#, which I’d characterize as a typical “application programming” language that’s used when low level control isn’t necessary. C#’s types are named similarly to C’s: int, short, long and byte. int is 32 bits wide. Unsigned versions are obtained by prefixing the type with u. Subscripts and sizes use a plain int, and by default integers wrap on overflow.

sentences.en: Next up, C#, which I’d characterize as a typical “application programming” language that’s used when low level control isn’t necessary.
sentences.zh: 接下来是 C#，我将其描述为一种典型的“应用程序编程”语言，在不需要低级控制时使用。

sentences.en: C#’s types are named similarly to C’s: int, short, long and byte.
sentences.zh: C# 的类型命名与 C 的类似：int、short、long 和 byte。

sentences.en: int is 32 bits wide.
sentences.zh: int 是 32 位宽。

sentences.en: Unsigned versions are obtained by prefixing the type with u.
sentences.zh: 无符号版本是通过在类型前加上 u 前缀来获得的。

sentences.en: Subscripts and sizes use a plain int, and by default integers wrap on overflow.
sentences.zh: 下标和大小使用普通 int，默认情况下整数在溢出时换行。

blocks.html_en: Go is a contemporary alternative to languages in the same domain as C#. Here we see the beginning of a trend: rather than using unintuitive C-style integer type names, Go just appends the bitwidth of each type: int8, uint64, etc. Another interesting difference crops up in the size of int and uint: they match the size of an address on the target platform, rather than being hardcoded to a particular width. Again, sizes and subscripts use int. Go defines integer overflow to wrap.

sentences.en: Go is a contemporary alternative to languages in the same domain as C#.
sentences.zh: Go 是与 C# 同一领域的语言的当代替代品。

sentences.en: Here we see the beginning of a trend: rather than using unintuitive C-style integer type names, Go just appends the bitwidth of each type: int8, uint64, etc. Another interesting difference crops up in the size of int and uint: they match the size of an address on the target platform, rather than being hardcoded to a particular width.
sentences.zh: 这里我们看到了一个趋势的开始：Go 没有使用不直观的 C​​ 风格整数类型名称，而是只是附加每种类型的位宽：int8、uint64 等。另一个有趣的区别出现在 int 和 uint 的大小上：它们匹配目标平台上地址的大小，而不是硬编码为特定宽度。

sentences.en: Again, sizes and subscripts use int.
sentences.zh: 同样，大小和下标使用 int。

sentences.en: Go defines integer overflow to wrap.
sentences.zh: Go 定义了整数溢出来换行。

blocks.html_en: Swift is yet another recent language in this “compiled statically-typed non-low-level commercial software” niche, and its integer types are similar to Go in several ways. Type names are identical to Go except for capitalization: Int8, UInt64, etc. Int and UInt have the same sizing behavior as Go, and subscripts & sizes both use Int. Curiously, Swift defaults to panicking on integer overflow, even in release builds.

sentences.en: Swift is yet another recent language in this “compiled statically-typed non-low-level commercial software” niche, and its integer types are similar to Go in several ways.
sentences.zh: Swift 是“编译的静态类型非低级商业软件”领域的另一种新语言，它的整数类型在很多方面与 Go 相似。

sentences.en: Type names are identical to Go except for capitalization: Int8, UInt64, etc. Int and UInt have the same sizing behavior as Go, and subscripts & sizes both use Int.
sentences.zh: 除了大小写外，类型名称与 Go 相同：Int8、UInt64 等。Int 和 UInt 具有与 Go 相同的大小调整行为，下标和大小都使用 Int。

sentences.en: Curiously, Swift defaults to panicking on integer overflow, even in release builds.
sentences.zh: 奇怪的是，即使在发布版本中，Swift 默认也会对整数溢出感到恐慌。

blocks.html_en: Rust: a better approach

sentences.en: Rust: a better approach
sentences.zh: Rust：更好的方法

blocks.html_en: Did you notice a commonality in all the examples I listed above? All those languages have some kind of default int type. They require extra typing to use unsigned types instead of signed ones and explicitly-sized types instead of the default-sized int. I’d say this encourages the use of int over other types. After all: would you rather stare at a screenful of ints or uint32_ts? This goes beyond aesthetics, too. It’s so easy to take the lazy way out and type int without thinking, rather than scrutinize the use-case to identify the best type for the job.

sentences.zh: 您是否注意到我上面列出的所有示例的共同点？

sentences.en: All those languages have some kind of default int type.
sentences.zh: 所有这些语言都有某种默认的 int 类型。

sentences.en: They require extra typing to use unsigned types instead of signed ones and explicitly-sized types instead of the default-sized int.
sentences.zh: 它们需要额外的输入来使用无符号类型而不是带符号类型，以及显式大小的类型而不是默认大小的 int。

sentences.en: I’d say this encourages the use of int over other types.
sentences.zh: 我想说这鼓励使用 int 而不是其他类型。

sentences.en: After all: would you rather stare at a screenful of ints or uint32_ts?
sentences.zh: 毕竟：您愿意盯着满屏的 int 还是 uint32_t？

sentences.en: This goes beyond aesthetics, too.
sentences.zh: 这也超出了美学的范围。

sentences.en: It’s so easy to take the lazy way out and type int without thinking, rather than scrutinize the use-case to identify the best type for the job.
sentences.zh: 我们很容易采取偷懒的方式，不假思索地输入 int，而不是仔细检查用例来确定最适合工作的类型。

blocks.html_en: Rust eliminates each of these points: all integer types have an explicit size, and all types are prefixed with either i or u for signed and unsigned respectively. There’s no bias towards a certain size or signedness. You’re forced to consider the circumstances and make a judgement on what type is most appropriate to the situation. Maybe memory is conserved as integers are narrowed, and bugs are prevented as – to invoke a dogma oft-cited by the Rust community – invalid values (negative numbers) become unrepresentable.

sentences.en: Rust eliminates each of these points: all integer types have an explicit size, and all types are prefixed with either i or u for signed and unsigned respectively.
sentences.zh: Rust 消除了这些要点：所有整数类型都有明确的大小，并且所有类型都以 i 或 u 为前缀，分别表示有符号和无符号。

sentences.en: There’s no bias towards a certain size or signedness.

sentences.en: You’re forced to consider the circumstances and make a judgement on what type is most appropriate to the situation.
sentences.zh: 您被迫考虑具体情况并判断哪种类型最适合该情况。

sentences.en: Maybe memory is conserved as integers are narrowed, and bugs are prevented as – to invoke a dogma oft-cited by the Rust community – invalid values (negative numbers) become unrepresentable.
sentences.zh: 也许随着整数的缩小，内存得到了保留，并且错误也被防止了——引用 Rust 社区经常引用的一条教条——无效值（负数）变得无法表示。

blocks.html_en: One thing you might notice about this table is the presence of usize and isize. usize is used for subscripts and sizes, just like size_t in C. Contrary to C, Rust doesn’t have implicit integer casting, so you really do have to use usize for your array indexes (unless you want to drown in as-casts). Using usize rather than a 32-bit int means arrays can have more than two billion elements, and using an unsigned type means negative array indexes can never occur.

sentences.en: Using usize rather than a 32-bit int means arrays can have more than two billion elements, and using an unsigned type means negative array indexes can never occur.
sentences.zh: 使用 usize 而不是 32 位 int 意味着数组可以拥有超过 20 亿个元素，而使用无符号类型意味着永远不会出现负数组索引。

blocks.html_en: There is a caveat to what I said about Rust not being biased to a particular integer type:

blocks.html_en: Rust defaults to i32 when it has no type information for an integer literal, so there is an (albeit minor) bias to signed 32-bit integers. In my experience of Rust, though, I found I used signed integers so rarely that I’d almost advocate for making unsigned integers the default. If Rust didn’t put unsigned and signed integers on equal footing I’d likely have never reached this conclusion.

blocks.html_en: When I started experimenting with C#, Swift and Go I quickly became frustrated. With my attitude of picking the right integer type for the task, I attempted to use uint for array indexes everywhere instead of the signed int these languages default to. It was impractical due to the amount of casting required, so I gave up in frustration. This just made me more certain: why can’t every language have integer types like Rust?

blocks.html_en: Why does C make signed integer overflow UB, anyway?

sentences.en: Why does C make signed integer overflow UB, anyway?
sentences.zh: 无论如何，为什么 C 会使有符号整数溢出 UB？

blocks.html_en: Originally the reason was differences in hardware behavior, but today the argument you’ll hear most often is performance. But don’t most CPUs perform wrapping when an arithmetic operation overflows, anyway? Why not just define overflow to wrap (at no extra cost, since it comes with the CPU “for free”) and be done with it?

sentences.en: Originally the reason was differences in hardware behavior, but today the argument you’ll hear most often is performance.
sentences.zh: 最初的原因是硬件行为的差异，但今天您最常听到的争论是性能。

sentences.en: But don’t most CPUs perform wrapping when an arithmetic operation overflows, anyway?
sentences.zh: 但大多数 CPU 不是在算术运算溢出时执行回绕吗？

sentences.en: Why not just define overflow to wrap (at no extra cost, since it comes with the CPU “for free”) and be done with it?
sentences.zh: 为什么不直接定义溢出来包装（无需额外成本，因为它“免费”随 CPU 一起提供）并完成它？

blocks.html_en: I watched a talk by Chandler Carruth on the topic of UB, in which he includes an enlightening example. (We’ll assume we’re running on an architecture with 64-bit addresses.1)

sentences.en: I watched a talk by Chandler Carruth on the topic of UB, in which he includes an enlightening example.
sentences.zh: 我观看了 Chandler Carruth 关于 UB 主题的演讲，其中他举了一个很有启发性的例子。

sentences.en: (We’ll assume we’re running on an architecture with 64-bit addresses.1)
sentences.zh: （我们假设我们正在具有 64 位地址的架构上运行。1）

blocks.html_en: The compiler is smart enough to eliminate the mutation of i1 and i2. Bytes are loaded directly from block + i1 and block + i2, then block + i1 + 1 and block + i2 + 1, then block + i1 + 2 and block + i2 + 2, and so on. These address calculations are done as part of the load instruction itself, so they occur within the space of a 64-bit integer.

sentences.en: The compiler is smart enough to eliminate the mutation of i1 and i2.
sentences.zh: 编译器足够聪明，可以消除 i1 和 i2 的突变。

sentences.en: Bytes are loaded directly from block + i1 and block + i2, then block + i1 + 1 and block + i2 + 1, then block + i1 + 2 and block + i2 + 2, and so on.
sentences.zh: 字节直接从 block + i1 和 block + i2 加载，然后是 block + i1 + 1 和 block + i2 + 1，然后是 block + i1 + 2 和 block + i2 + 2，依此类推。

sentences.en: These address calculations are done as part of the load instruction itself, so they occur within the space of a 64-bit integer.
sentences.zh: 这些地址计算是作为加载指令本身的一部分完成的，因此它们发生在 64 位整数的空间内。

blocks.html_en: Say we had forced the compiler to wrap on overflow. i1++; and i2++; will wrap when they reach the limit of a 32-bit integer. To ensure the runtime behavior is faithful to this wrapping, the compiler generates extra instructions which make block + i1 + n wrap back around to block + 0 when i1 + n overflows 32 bits. In other words, “the CPU does wrapping on overflow for free” doesn’t hold in this case, and we get bloated code as a result.

sentences.en: Say we had forced the compiler to wrap on overflow.
sentences.zh: 假设我们强制编译器在溢出时换行。

sentences.en: i1++; and i2++; will wrap when they reach the limit of a 32-bit integer.
sentences.zh: i1++；和 i2++；当它们达到 32 位整数的限制时将换行。

sentences.en: To ensure the runtime behavior is faithful to this wrapping, the compiler generates extra instructions which make block + i1 + n wrap back around to block + 0 when i1 + n overflows 32 bits.
sentences.zh: 为了确保运行时行为忠实于这种包装，编译器会生成额外的指令，当 i1 + n 溢出 32 位时，这些指令使 block + i1 + n 包装回 block + 0。

sentences.en: In other words, “the CPU does wrapping on overflow for free” doesn’t hold in this case, and we get bloated code as a result.
sentences.zh: 换句话说，“CPU 在溢出时免费包装”在这种情况下并不成立，结果我们得到了臃肿的代码。

blocks.html_en: In recent years I’ve been convinced that unsigned sizes were a serious error, probably even one of the great early computing mistakes, and that sizes and subscripts should be signed. Not only that, pkg-config has no business dealing with gigantic objects! We’re talking about short strings and tiny files. If it ends up with a large object, then there’s a defect somewhere — either in itself or the system — and it should abort. Therefore sizes and subscripts are a natural int!

sentences.en: In recent years I’ve been convinced that unsigned sizes were a serious error, probably even one of the great early computing mistakes, and that sizes and subscripts should be signed.
sentences.zh: 近年来，我一直坚信无符号的大小是一个严重的错误，甚至可能是早期最大的计算错误之一，并且大小和下标应该被签名。

sentences.en: Not only that, pkg-config has no business dealing with gigantic objects!
sentences.zh: 不仅如此，pkg-config 不适合处理巨大的对象！

sentences.en: We’re talking about short strings and tiny files.
sentences.zh: 我们谈论的是短字符串和小文件。

sentences.en: If it ends up with a large object, then there’s a defect somewhere — either in itself or the system — and it should abort.
sentences.zh: 如果它最终得到一个大对象，那么某个地方就存在缺陷——无论是本身还是系统——并且它应该中止。

sentences.en: Therefore sizes and subscripts are a natural int!
sentences.zh: 因此大小和下标是一个自然的整数！

blocks.html_en: See that link in the quoted paragraph? It links to a short document written by none other than Bjarne Stroustrop, who also says that sizes and subscripts should be signed. With a skeptical attitude and my Rust-based preconceptions I didn’t find his arguments very convincing. Still, there was a niggling feeling in the back of my mind that all these people must have a point.

sentences.en: See that link in the quoted paragraph?

sentences.zh: 它链接到由 Bjarne Stroustrop 撰写的简短文档，他还表示应该在尺寸和下标上签名。

sentences.en: Still, there was a niggling feeling in the back of my mind that all these people must have a point.
sentences.zh: 尽管如此，我内心还是有一种不安的感觉，所有这些人一定都有道理。

blocks.html_en: Looking at some C alternatives

sentences.en: Looking at some C alternatives
sentences.zh: 寻找一些 C 替代方案

blocks.html_en: Odin is one of a fray of up-and-coming C alternatives which focuses on simplicity and “the joy of programming”. After some failed experimentation with another C alternative, Zig, I decided to take a closer look at Odin.

sentences.en: Odin is one of a fray of up-and-coming C alternatives which focuses on simplicity and “the joy of programming”.
sentences.zh: Odin 是众多新兴的 C 替代品之一，它注重简单性和“编程的乐趣”。

sentences.en: After some failed experimentation with another C alternative, Zig, I decided to take a closer look at Odin.
sentences.zh: 在尝试了另一种 C 语言 Zig 失败后，我决定仔细研究一下 Odin。

blocks.html_en: Once again, the familiar “modern language” integer system appears, with a few twists oriented towards low-level programming:

sentences.en: Once again, the familiar “modern language” integer system appears, with a few twists oriented towards low-level programming:
sentences.zh: 熟悉的“现代语言”整数系统再次出现，并针对低级编程进行了一些修改：

blocks.html_en: • int and uint are address-sized

sentences.en: int and uint are address-sized
sentences.zh: int 和 uint 是地址大小的

blocks.html_en: • i8 through to i128 are signed

sentences.en: i8 through to i128 are signed
sentences.zh: i8 到 i128 均已签名

blocks.html_en: • u8 through to u128 are unsigned

sentences.en: u8 through to u128 are unsigned
sentences.zh: u8 到 u128 都是无符号的

blocks.html_en: • explicitly-sized types can be suffixed with either le or be to make their in-memory representation little-endian or big-endian

sentences.en: explicitly-sized types can be suffixed with either le or be to make their in-memory representation little-endian or big-endian
sentences.zh: 显式大小的类型可以以 le 或 be 为后缀，以使其在内存中表示为小端或大端

blocks.html_en: • sizes and subscripts use int

sentences.en: sizes and subscripts use int
sentences.zh: 大小和下标使用 int

blocks.html_en: • wrap on overflow

sentences.en: wrap on overflow
sentences.zh: 溢出时换行

blocks.html_en: This time, I decided to have an open mind and take a closer look at what benefits this setup might have. Some of the arguments below aren’t mine, but I can’t remember which ones I picked up from where and which ones are my own.

sentences.en: This time, I decided to have an open mind and take a closer look at what benefits this setup might have.
sentences.zh: 这一次，我决定以开放的态度并仔细研究这种设置可能带来的好处。

sentences.en: Some of the arguments below aren’t mine, but I can’t remember which ones I picked up from where and which ones are my own.
sentences.zh: 下面的一些论点不是我的，但我不记得哪些是我从哪里拿起的，哪些是我自己的。

blocks.html_en: Address-sized is the perfect default size

sentences.en: Address-sized is the perfect default size
sentences.zh: 地址大小是完美的默认大小

blocks.html_en: As we saw earlier, mixing pointers and 32-bit integers can lead to performance problems if we aren’t willing to make overflow UB. Had we made i1 and i2 be of type ptrdiff_t or something similar, then wrapping on overflow would’ve worked just fine. Moreover, ISAs such as A64 only have 32- and 64-bit arithmetic instructions, so operating on 8- and 16-bit integers requires an extra and instruction. Performing all arithmetic on address-sized integers is generally the best option.

sentences.en: As we saw earlier, mixing pointers and 32-bit integers can lead to performance problems if we aren’t willing to make overflow UB.
sentences.zh: 正如我们之前看到的，如果我们不愿意造成溢出 UB，混合指针和 32 位整数可能会导致性能问题。

sentences.en: Had we made i1 and i2 be of type ptrdiff_t or something similar, then wrapping on overflow would’ve worked just fine.
sentences.zh: 如果我们将 i1 和 i2 设为 ptrdiff_t 或类似的类型，那么溢出包装就会很好地工作。

sentences.en: Moreover, ISAs such as A64 only have 32- and 64-bit arithmetic instructions, so operating on 8- and 16-bit integers requires an extra and instruction.
sentences.zh: 此外，A64等ISA仅具有32位和64位算术指令，因此对8位和16位整数进行操作需要额外的and指令。

sentences.en: Performing all arithmetic on address-sized integers is generally the best option.
sentences.zh: 对地址大小的整数执行所有算术通常是最佳选择。

blocks.html_en: One counterargument to this is that modern CPUs are usually memory-bound, so packing data structures to be as small as possible is a good idea. Making integers address-sized rather than defaulting to, say, 32 bits, goes against this goal. Regardless, I’d say the simplicity of being able to use a plain int for array indexes, sizes, arithmetic, and as a default integer type outweigh the cost of having to remember to use int32 where appropriate.

sentences.en: One counterargument to this is that modern CPUs are usually memory-bound, so packing data structures to be as small as possible is a good idea.
sentences.zh: 对此的一个反驳是，现代 CPU 通常受内存限制，因此将数据结构打包得尽可能小是一个好主意。

sentences.en: Making integers address-sized rather than defaulting to, say, 32 bits, goes against this goal.
sentences.zh: 将整数设置为地址大小而不是默认为 32 位，这违背了这一目标。

sentences.en: Regardless, I’d say the simplicity of being able to use a plain int for array indexes, sizes, arithmetic, and as a default integer type outweigh the cost of having to remember to use int32 where appropriate.
sentences.zh: 无论如何，我想说的是，能够使用普通 int 来表示数组索引、大小、算术以及作为默认整数类型的简单性超过了必须记住在适当的情况下使用 int32 的成本。

blocks.html_en: In release builds, wrapping on overflow is a fine tradeoff

sentences.en: In release builds, wrapping on overflow is a fine tradeoff
sentences.zh: 在发布版本中，溢出包装是一个很好的权衡

blocks.html_en: Making overflow undefined behavior has no benefit2 if all integers being operated on are address-sized. I’d rather have a couple casts at the top of a function than more UB time bombs waiting to explode.

sentences.en: Making overflow undefined behavior has no benefit2 if all integers being operated on are address-sized.
sentences.zh: 如果所有正在操作的整数都是地址大小的，那么使溢出行为未定义没有任何好处2。

sentences.en: I’d rather have a couple casts at the top of a function than more UB time bombs waiting to explode.
sentences.zh: 我宁愿在一个函数的顶部有几个强制转换，也不愿有更多的 UB 定时炸弹等待爆炸。

blocks.html_en: Given the roughly 30% overhead of overflow checks, I think restricting them to debug builds is a reasonable tradeoff. I can also see an argument for using wrap on overflow even in debug builds for consistency and simplicity.

sentences.en: Given the roughly 30% overhead of overflow checks, I think restricting them to debug builds is a reasonable tradeoff.
sentences.zh: 考虑到溢出检查大约 30% 的开销，我认为将它们限制为调试构建是一个合理的权衡。

sentences.en: I can also see an argument for using wrap on overflow even in debug builds for consistency and simplicity.
sentences.zh: 我还可以看到即使在调试版本中也为了一致性和简单性而使用溢出换行的争论。

blocks.html_en: Unsigned integers are unintuitive

sentences.en: Unsigned integers are unintuitive
sentences.zh: 无符号整数不直观

blocks.html_en: Let’s say you want to loop from a number down to zero. It’s trivial with signed integers:

sentences.en: Let’s say you want to loop from a number down to zero.
sentences.zh: 假设您想从数字循环到零。

sentences.en: It’s trivial with signed integers:
sentences.zh: 对于有符号整数来说这很简单：

blocks.html_en: As you’d expect, running demo(3) prints

sentences.en: As you’d expect, running demo(3) prints
sentences.zh: 正如您所期望的，运行 demo(3) 会打印

blocks.html_en: i and top are never negative here, though, so surely unsigned integers are better in these circumstances?

sentences.en: i and top are never negative here, though, so surely unsigned integers are better in these circumstances?
sentences.zh: 不过，这里 i 和 top 永远不会是负数，所以在这些情况下无符号整数肯定更好吗？

blocks.html_en: This is an endless loop. For an unsigned integer n, n >= 0 is always true. Since unsigned integers wrap on overflow, when i reaches zero we decrement it and reach 4,294,967,295. To fix this we can change the condition to i <= top:

sentences.en: This is an endless loop.
sentences.zh: 这是一个无限循环。

sentences.en: For an unsigned integer n, n >= 0 is always true.
sentences.zh: 对于无符号整数 n，n >= 0 始终为真。

sentences.en: Since unsigned integers wrap on overflow, when i reaches zero we decrement it and reach 4,294,967,295.
sentences.zh: 由于无符号整数在溢出时换行，因此当 i 达到零时，我们将其递减并达到 4,294,967,295。

sentences.en: To fix this we can change the condition to i <= top:
sentences.zh: 为了解决这个问题，我们可以将条件更改为 i <= top：

blocks.html_en: Once i has wrapped around, it will be greater than top and as such break out of the loop. Just awful.

sentences.en: Once i has wrapped around, it will be greater than top and as such break out of the loop.
sentences.zh: 一旦 i 绕回，它将大于 top 并因此跳出循环。

sentences.en: Just awful.
sentences.zh: 太糟糕了。

blocks.html_en: Won’t signed integers make bounds checks slower?

sentences.en: Won’t signed integers make bounds checks slower?
sentences.zh: 有符号整数不会使边界检查变慢吗？

blocks.html_en: Performing a bounds check when using signed indexes requires checking both 0 <= i and i < count. These two checks are independent from one another, and so a contemporary CPU can run them in parallel. Rarely are all the ALUs of a CPU saturated, so removing the first of these checks likely won’t change how long the bounds check takes to execute.

sentences.en: Performing a bounds check when using signed indexes requires checking both 0 <= i and i < count.
sentences.zh: 使用带符号索引时执行边界检查需要检查 0 <= i 和 i < count。

sentences.en: These two checks are independent from one another, and so a contemporary CPU can run them in parallel.
sentences.zh: 这两项检查是相互独立的，因此现代 CPU 可以并行运行它们。

sentences.en: Rarely are all the ALUs of a CPU saturated, so removing the first of these checks likely won’t change how long the bounds check takes to execute.
sentences.zh: CPU 的所有 ALU 很少都饱和，因此删除第一个检查可能不会改变边界检查执行所需的时间。

blocks.html_en: Unsigned integers are more bug-prone

sentences.en: Unsigned integers are more bug-prone
sentences.zh: 无符号整数更容易出现错误

blocks.html_en: Suppose we have two array indexes, i and j. Suppose also that the overhead of panicking on overflow is unacceptable for our use-case, so we’re wrapping on overflow. We wish to find how many elements lie between i and j, and want to copy them to a new array. Simple, right? j - i is all that’s needed to determine the number of elements, and then we can create a new array of that size and perform the copy.

sentences.en: Suppose we have two array indexes, i and j.
sentences.zh: 假设我们有两个数组索引，i 和 j。

sentences.en: Suppose also that the overhead of panicking on overflow is unacceptable for our use-case, so we’re wrapping on overflow.
sentences.zh: 还假设溢出恐慌的开销对于我们的用例来说是不可接受的，因此我们要对溢出进行包装。

sentences.en: We wish to find how many elements lie between i and j, and want to copy them to a new array.
sentences.zh: 我们希望找到 i 和 j 之间有多少个元素，并将它们复制到一个新数组中。

sentences.en: Simple, right?
sentences.zh: 很简单，对吧？

sentences.en: j - i is all that’s needed to determine the number of elements, and then we can create a new array of that size and perform the copy.
sentences.zh: j - i 足以确定元素数量，然后我们可以创建该大小的新数组并执行复制。

blocks.html_en: Imagine that j is always greater than or equal to i in our test data, but somehow ends up less than i in production. If i and j are unsigned, j - i will wrap around to what is probably a very large number. We end up creating an array of that very large size; if we’re lucky the copy has bounds checks and causes a panic.

sentences.en: Imagine that j is always greater than or equal to i in our test data, but somehow ends up less than i in production.
sentences.zh: 想象一下，在我们的测试数据中，j 始终大于或等于 i，但在生产中却以某种方式最终小于 i。

sentences.en: If i and j are unsigned, j - i will wrap around to what is probably a very large number.
sentences.zh: 如果 i 和 j 是无符号的，j - i 将环绕到可能是一个非常大的数字。

sentences.en: We end up creating an array of that very large size; if we’re lucky the copy has bounds checks and causes a panic.
sentences.zh: 我们最终创建了一个非常大的数组；如果我们幸运的话，副本会进行边界检查并引起恐慌。

blocks.html_en: The point is that an unsigned underflow is hard to catch: there’s no way to tell whether a large number was erroneously caused by wrapping or if its presence is intentional. Using signed integers here means the invalid j - i subtraction produces a “poisoned” (negative) result immediately. This result can be caught straight away when it’s used to create an array, rather than later on through the unpredictable flow-on effects of huge numbers.

sentences.en: The point is that an unsigned underflow is hard to catch: there’s no way to tell whether a large number was erroneously caused by wrapping or if its presence is intentional.
sentences.zh: 关键是无符号下溢很难捕获：无法判断大量数字是否是由回绕错误引起的，或者它的存在是否是故意的。

sentences.en: Using signed integers here means the invalid j - i subtraction produces a “poisoned” (negative) result immediately.
sentences.zh: 这里使用有符号整数意味着无效的 j - i 减法立即产生“中毒”（负）结果。

sentences.en: This result can be caught straight away when it’s used to create an array, rather than later on through the unpredictable flow-on effects of huge numbers.
sentences.zh: 这个结果可以在用于创建数组时立即捕获，而不是稍后通过巨大数字的不可预测的流动效应来捕获。

blocks.html_en: Won’t signed integers require more asserts?

sentences.en: Won’t signed integers require more asserts?
sentences.zh: 有符号整数不需要更多断言吗？

blocks.html_en: It is true that to have caught that negative array size our array allocation code would’ve needed an assert(count > 0). However, to have caught it if we had been using unsigned integers requires much more than just an assert – we’d need to enable comprehensive overflow checking for the entire program.

sentences.en: It is true that to have caught that negative array size our array allocation code would’ve needed an assert(count > 0).
sentences.zh: 确实，要捕获负数组大小，我们的数组分配代码需要一个断言（count > 0）。

sentences.en: However, to have caught it if we had been using unsigned integers requires much more than just an assert – we’d need to enable comprehensive overflow checking for the entire program.
sentences.zh: 然而，如果我们一直使用无符号整数，要捕获它需要的不仅仅是断言——我们需要对整个程序启用全面的溢出检查。

blocks.html_en: You can view the asserts you need to add when using signed integers as a lightweight form of overflow checking. Rather than testing for overflow after every single arithmetic operation, we can just put an equivalent check at the top of every function. In other words, a large number of overflow checks have been coalesced into a single check at an API boundary.

sentences.en: You can view the asserts you need to add when using signed integers as a lightweight form of overflow checking.
sentences.zh: 您可以查看在使用有符号整数作为溢出检查的轻量级形式时需要添加的断言。

sentences.en: Rather than testing for overflow after every single arithmetic operation, we can just put an equivalent check at the top of every function.
sentences.zh: 我们可以在每个函数的顶部进行等效检查，而不是在每次算术运算后测试溢出。

sentences.en: In other words, a large number of overflow checks have been coalesced into a single check at an API boundary.
sentences.zh: 换句话说，大量的溢出检查已合并为 API 边界处的单个检查。

blocks.html_en: Now, of course a single assert(count > 0) isn’t equivalent to thoroughly checking for overflows everywhere. For instance, it wouldn’t catch something like create_array(1000 + j - i). There’s a tradeoff between performance and safety here, and I’d say that signed integers with liberal usage of asserts and wrapping on overflow is a happy middle-ground.

sentences.en: Now, of course a single assert(count > 0) isn’t equivalent to thoroughly checking for overflows everywhere.
sentences.zh: 当然，单个断言（count > 0）并不等于彻底检查各处的溢出。

sentences.en: For instance, it wouldn’t catch something like create_array(1000 + j - i).
sentences.zh: 例如，它不会捕获类似 create_array(1000 + j - i) 的内容。

sentences.en: There’s a tradeoff between performance and safety here, and I’d say that signed integers with liberal usage of asserts and wrapping on overflow is a happy middle-ground.
sentences.zh: 这里需要在性能和安全性之间进行权衡，我想说，自由使用断言和溢出包装的有符号整数是一个令人满意的中间立场。

blocks.html_en: Don’t signed integers have a lower maximum value, so you’ll need to use more bits?

sentences.en: Don’t signed integers have a lower maximum value, so you’ll need to use more bits?
sentences.zh: 有符号整数的最大值不是较低，因此您需要使用更多位吗？

blocks.html_en: I’d disagree with this. Given we’re on a 64-bit platform, being limited to 63 bits’ worth of elements in an array really doesn’t matter, and if anything is probably a good thing. For instance, Rust limits the size of all allocations to the maximum of a signed address-sized integer so APIs like ptr::offset(isize) can function.

sentences.en: I’d disagree with this.
sentences.zh: 我不同意这一点。

sentences.en: Given we’re on a 64-bit platform, being limited to 63 bits’ worth of elements in an array really doesn’t matter, and if anything is probably a good thing.
sentences.zh: 考虑到我们使用的是 64 位平台，数组中的元素限制为 63 位实际上并不重要，而且如果有的话可能是一件好事。

sentences.en: For instance, Rust limits the size of all allocations to the maximum of a signed address-sized integer so APIs like ptr::offset(isize) can function.
sentences.zh: 例如，Rust 将所有分配的大小限制为有符号地址大小的整数的最大值，以便像 ptr::offset(isize) 这样的 API 可以运行。

blocks.html_en: For smaller bit-widths, like if we’re using 32-bit indexes into an array for compactness, losing one bit won’t make much of a difference anyway. I don’t know of any applications where a maximum of two billion elements isn’t sufficient, but four billion is.

sentences.en: For smaller bit-widths, like if we’re using 32-bit indexes into an array for compactness, losing one bit won’t make much of a difference anyway.
sentences.zh: 对于较小的位宽，例如我们在数组中使用 32 位索引以实现紧凑性，丢失一位无论如何也不会产生太大影响。

sentences.en: I don’t know of any applications where a maximum of two billion elements isn’t sufficient, but four billion is.
sentences.zh: 我不知道有哪个应用程序最多 20 亿个元素是不够的，但 40 亿个元素就足够了。

blocks.html_en: • I am aware that most “64-bit” CPUs actually have 48- or 52-bit addresses. Let’s just go with “64-bit addresses” for simplicity. ↩︎

sentences.en: I am aware that most “64-bit” CPUs actually have 48- or 52-bit addresses.
sentences.zh: 我知道大多数“64 位”CPU 实际上有 48 位或 52 位地址。

sentences.en: Let’s just go with “64-bit addresses” for simplicity. ↩︎
sentences.zh: 为了简单起见，我们只使用“64 位地址”。 ↩︎

blocks.html_en: I am aware that most “64-bit” CPUs actually have 48- or 52-bit addresses. Let’s just go with “64-bit addresses” for simplicity. ↩︎

sentences.en: I am aware that most “64-bit” CPUs actually have 48- or 52-bit addresses.
sentences.zh: 我知道大多数“64 位”CPU 实际上有 48 位或 52 位地址。

sentences.en: Let’s just go with “64-bit addresses” for simplicity. ↩︎
sentences.zh: 为了简单起见，我们只使用“64 位地址”。 ↩︎

blocks.html_en: • Overflow checks are an exception. Making overflow UB means the compiler can eliminate overflow checks, potentially improving performance. However, I’d argue that this is yet another case of C prioritizing performance over correctness, and that those checks shouldn’t be removed in the first place. ↩︎

sentences.en: Overflow checks are an exception.

sentences.en: Making overflow UB means the compiler can eliminate overflow checks, potentially improving performance.

content_html_zh:
在这篇文章中，我将阐述我对整数类型的看法，以及我是如何得出这些观点的。 希望你一开始不同意，最后被说服！

现状

让我们从我所描述的当今流行编程语言的现状开始。 但首先，有一些警告：出于显而易见的原因，我不会在本文中提及脚本语言。 此外，我将 Java 排除在本节之外，因为它在整数方面有一些奇怪的地方（int 与 Integer，并且没有明显的无符号类型）。

我们将从经典的系统编程语言 C 开始。 除了传统的 C 整数类型 int、char、short、long、long long 等之外，C99 还添加了固定大小类型，名称为 int8_t、uint64_t 等。 在现代 64 位平台上，int 始终是有符号的且宽度为 32 位。 此外，C 还有一组令人困惑的机器相关类型，例如 ptrdiff_t 和 size_t。 一般来说，下标和大小的类型为 size_t，但 C 的隐式转换意味着您经常会看到使用 int 或其他类型。 当有符号整数在 C 中溢出时，它们会调用未定义的行为，这意味着编译器假设溢出永远不会发生。 另一方面，无符号整数的溢出被定义为换行。

接下来是 C#，我将其描述为一种典型的“应用程序编程”语言，在不需要低级控制时使用。 C# 的类型命名与 C 的类似：int、short、long 和 byte。 int 是 32 位宽。 无符号版本是通过在类型前加上 u 前缀来获得的。 下标和大小使用普通 int，默认情况下整数在溢出时换行。

Go 是与 C# 同一领域的语言的当代替代品。 这里我们看到了一个趋势的开始：Go 没有使用不直观的 C​​ 风格整数类型名称，而是只是附加每种类型的位宽：int8、uint64 等。另一个有趣的区别出现在 int 和 uint 的大小上：它们匹配目标平台上地址的大小，而不是硬编码为特定宽度。 同样，大小和下标使用 int。 Go 定义了整数溢出来换行。

Swift 是“编译的静态类型非低级商业软件”领域的另一种新语言，它的整数类型在很多方面与 Go 相似。 除了大小写外，类型名称与 Go 相同：Int8、UInt64 等。Int 和 UInt 具有与 Go 相同的大小调整行为，下标和大小都使用 Int。 奇怪的是，即使在发布版本中，Swift 默认也会对整数溢出感到恐慌。

Rust：更好的方法

您是否注意到我上面列出的所有示例的共同点？ 所有这些语言都有某种默认的 int 类型。 它们需要额外的输入来使用无符号类型而不是带符号类型，以及显式大小的类型而不是默认大小的 int。 我想说这鼓励使用 int 而不是其他类型。 毕竟：您愿意盯着满屏的 int 还是 uint32_t？ 这也超出了美学的范围。 我们很容易采取偷懒的方式，不假思索地输入 int，而不是仔细检查用例来确定最适合工作的类型。

Rust 消除了这些要点：所有整数类型都有明确的大小，并且所有类型都以 i 或 u 为前缀，分别表示有符号和无符号。 不存在对特定大小或符号的偏见。 您被迫考虑具体情况并判断哪种类型最适合该情况。 也许随着整数的缩小，内存得到了保留，并且错误也被防止了——引用 Rust 社区经常引用的一条教条——无效值（负数）变得无法表示。

name签署？大小（以位为单位）
u8no8
u16no16
u32no32
u64no64
usizeno地址大小
i8yes8
i16yes16
i32yes32
i64yes64
isizeyes地址大小

关于此表，您可能会注意到的一件事是 usize 和 isize 的存在。 usize 用于下标和大小，就像 C 中的 size_t 一样。 与 C 相反，Rust 没有隐式整数转换，因此您确实必须使用 usize 作为数组索引（除非您想淹没在 as-cast 中）。 使用 usize 而不是 32 位 int 意味着数组可以拥有超过 20 亿个元素，而使用无符号类型意味着永远不会出现负数组索引。

Rust 在调试版本中因溢出而恐慌以捕获错误，但在发布版本中使用包装来提高性能。 请注意溢出时缺少 UB。

我所说的 Rust 不偏向于特定的整数类型有一个警告：

fn demo() {
	let x = 1; // x: i32
}

当 Rust 没有整数文字的类型信息时，它默认为 i32，因此对有符号 32 位整数存在（尽管较小）偏差。 不过，根据我使用 Rust 的经验，我发现我很少使用有符号整数，以至于我几乎提倡将无符号整数设置为默认值。 如果 Rust 没有将无符号和有符号整数放在同等地位，我可能永远不会得出这个结论。

对我来说，所有这些变化似乎都是明显的改进。

Rust 程序员尝试其他语言

当我开始尝试 C#、Swift 和 Go 时，我很快就感到沮丧。 出于为任务选择正确整数类型的态度，我尝试在任何地方使用 uint 作为数组索引，而不是这些语言默认使用的有符号 int。 由于需要大量的铸造工作，这是不切实际的，所以我沮丧地放弃了。 这让我更加确定：为什么每种语言不能像 Rust 一样拥有整数类型？

无论如何，为什么 C 会使有符号整数溢出 UB？

最初的原因是硬件行为的差异，但今天您最常听到的争论是性能。 但大多数 CPU 不是在算术运算溢出时执行回绕吗？ 为什么不直接定义溢出来包装（无需额外成本，因为它“免费”随 CPU 一起提供）并完成它？

我观看了 Chandler Carruth 关于 UB 主题的演讲，其中他举了一个很有启发性的例子。 （我们假设我们正在具有 64 位地址的架构上运行。1）

bool mainGtU(int32_t i1, int32_t i2, uint8_t *block)
{
	uint8_t c1, c2;

	c1 = block[i1]; c2 = block[i2];
	if (c1 != c2) return c1 > c2;
	i1++; i2++;

	c1 = block[i1]; c2 = block[i2];
	if (c1 != c2) return c1 > c2;
	i1++; i2++;

	// repeats several more times
}

编译器足够聪明，可以消除 i1 和 i2 的突变。 字节直接从 block + i1 和 block + i2 加载，然后是 block + i1 + 1 和 block + i2 + 1，然后是 block + i1 + 2 和 block + i2 + 2，依此类推。 这些地址计算是作为加载指令本身的一部分完成的，因此它们发生在 64 位整数的空间内。

假设我们强制编译器在溢出时换行。 i1++；和 i2++；当它们达到 32 位整数的限制时将换行。 为了确保运行时行为忠实于这种包装，编译器会生成额外的指令，当 i1 + n 溢出 32 位时，这些指令使 block + i1 + n 包装回 block + 0。 换句话说，“CPU 在溢出时免费包装”在这种情况下并不成立，结果我们得到了臃肿的代码。

这一切都不是假设的；使 i1 和 i2 无符号也会导致发出这些额外的指令。 在他的演讲中，Chandler 使用此代码作为示例，说明如何从无符号整数切换到有符号整数可以提高性能，因为它允许编译器在溢出时利用 UB。 他甚至这么说

如果您想对一个整数进行算术处理，而不是在某个模空间（2 的某个幂）中进行处理，请使用 Make。 它。 签署。 如果您需要更多位，请获取更多位。 保留签名。

这对我来说很有趣。 难道你不应该使用类型系统来防止错误吗？ 如果一个数字永远不可能是负数，为什么要让它带符号呢？ 钱德勒对这个问题的看法让我感到不舒服。

进一步进军 C

当我沉浸在使用 C 的系统编程中时，我遇到了一些人，他们一直说有符号整数应该用于索引，即使它们不能为负数。 特别是，我在 Chris Wellons 的博客上读到了三篇不同的文章，它们都支持这个观点：

近年来，我一直坚信无符号的大小是一个严重的错误，甚至可能是早期最大的计算错误之一，并且大小和下标应该被签名。 不仅如此，pkg-config 不适合处理巨大的对象！ 我们谈论的是短字符串和小文件。 如果它最终得到一个大对象，那么某个地方就存在缺陷——无论是本身还是系统——并且它应该中止。 因此大小和下标是一个自然的整数！

看到引用段落中的链接了吗？ 它链接到由 Bjarne Stroustrop 撰写的简短文档，他还表示应该在尺寸和下标上签名。 带着怀疑的态度和我对 Rust 的先入之见，我觉得他的论点不太令人信服。 尽管如此，我内心还是有一种不安的感觉，所有这些人一定都有道理。

寻找一些 C 替代方案

Odin 是众多新兴的 C 替代品之一，它注重简单性和“编程的乐趣”。 在尝试了另一种 C 语言 Zig 失败后，我决定仔细研究一下 Odin。

熟悉的“现代语言”整数系统再次出现，并针对低级编程进行了一些修改：

  • int 和 uint 是地址大小的
  • i8 到 i128 均已签名
  • u8 到 u128 都是无符号的
  • 显式大小的类型可以以 le 或 be 为后缀，以使其在内存中表示为小端或大端
  • 大小和下标使用 int
  • 溢出时换行

这一次，我决定以开放的态度并仔细研究这种设置可能带来的好处。 下面的一些论点不是我的，但我不记得哪些是我从哪里拿起的，哪些是我自己的。

地址大小是完美的默认大小

正如我们之前看到的，如果我们不愿意造成溢出 UB，混合指针和 32 位整数可能会导致性能问题。 如果我们将 i1 和 i2 设为 ptrdiff_t 或类似的类型，那么溢出包装就会很好地工作。 此外，A64等ISA仅具有32位和64位算术指令，因此对8位和16位整数进行操作需要额外的and指令。 对地址大小的整数执行所有算术通常是最佳选择。

对此的一个反驳是，现代 CPU 通常受内存限制，因此将数据结构打包得尽可能小是一个好主意。 将整数设置为地址大小而不是默认为 32 位，这违背了这一目标。 无论如何，我想说的是，能够使用普通 int 来表示数组索引、大小、算术以及作为默认整数类型的简单性超过了必须记住在适当的情况下使用 int32 的成本。

在发布版本中，溢出包装是一个很好的权衡

如果所有正在操作的整数都是地址大小的，那么使溢出行为未定义没有任何好处2。 我宁愿在一个函数的顶部有几个强制转换，也不愿有更多的 UB 定时炸弹等待爆炸。

考虑到溢出检查大约 30% 的开销，我认为将它们限制为调试构建是一个合理的权衡。 我还可以看到即使在调试版本中也为了一致性和简单性而使用溢出换行的争论。

无符号整数不直观

假设您想从数字循环到零。 对于有符号整数来说这很简单：

void demo(int32_t top)
{
	for (int32_t i = top; i >= 0; i--) {
		printf("%d\n", i);
	}
}

正如您所期望的，运行 demo(3) 会打印

3
2
1
0

不过，这里 i 和 top 永远不会是负数，所以在这些情况下无符号整数肯定更好吗？

void demo(uint32_t top)
{
	for (uint32_t i = top; i >= 0; i--) {
		printf("%u\n", i);
	}
}

这是一个无限循环。 对于无符号整数 n，n >= 0 始终为真。 由于无符号整数在溢出时换行，因此当 i 达到零时，我们将其递减并达到 4,294,967,295。 为了解决这个问题，我们可以将条件更改为 i <= top：

void demo(uint32_t top)
{
	for (uint32_t i = top; i <= top; i--) {
		printf("%u\n", i);
	}
}

一旦 i 绕回，它将大于 top 并因此跳出循环。 太糟糕了。

有符号整数不会使边界检查变慢吗？

使用带符号索引时执行边界检查需要检查 0 <= i 和 i < count。 这两项检查是相互独立的，因此现代 CPU 可以并行运行它们。 CPU 的所有 ALU 很少都饱和，因此删除第一个检查可能不会改变边界检查执行所需的时间。

无符号整数更容易出现错误

假设我们有两个数组索引，i 和 j。 还假设溢出恐慌的开销对于我们的用例来说是不可接受的，因此我们要对溢出进行包装。 我们希望找到 i 和 j 之间有多少个元素，并将它们复制到一个新数组中。 很简单，对吧？ j - i 足以确定元素数量，然后我们可以创建该大小的新数组并执行复制。

想象一下，在我们的测试数据中，j 始终大于或等于 i，但在生产中却以某种方式最终小于 i。 如果 i 和 j 是无符号的，j - i 将环绕到可能是一个非常大的数字。 我们最终创建了一个非常大的数组；如果我们幸运的话，副本会进行边界检查并引起恐慌。

关键是无符号下溢很难捕获：无法判断大量数字是否是由回绕错误引起的，或者它的存在是否是故意的。 这里使用有符号整数意味着无效的 j - i 减法立即产生“中毒”（负）结果。 这个结果可以在用于创建数组时立即捕获，而不是稍后通过巨大数字的不可预测的流动效应来捕获。

有符号整数不需要更多断言吗？

确实，要捕获负数组大小，我们的数组分配代码需要一个断言（count > 0）。 然而，如果我们一直使用无符号整数，要捕获它需要的不仅仅是断言——我们需要对整个程序启用全面的溢出检查。

您可以查看在使用有符号整数作为溢出检查的轻量级形式时需要添加的断言。 我们可以在每个函数的顶部进行等效检查，而不是在每次算术运算后测试溢出。 换句话说，大量的溢出检查已合并为 API 边界处的单个检查。

当然，单个断言（count > 0）并不等于彻底检查各处的溢出。 例如，它不会捕获类似 create_array(1000 + j - i) 的内容。 这里需要在性能和安全性之间进行权衡，我想说，自由使用断言和溢出包装的有符号整数是一个令人满意的中间立场。

有符号整数的最大值不是较低，因此您需要使用更多位吗？

我不同意这一点。 考虑到我们使用的是 64 位平台，数组中的元素限制为 63 位实际上并不重要，而且如果有的话可能是一件好事。 例如，Rust 将所有分配的大小限制为有符号地址大小的整数的最大值，以便像 ptr::offset(isize) 这样的 API 可以运行。

对于较小的位宽，例如我们在数组中使用 32 位索引以实现紧凑性，丢失一位无论如何也不会产生太大影响。 我不知道有哪个应用程序最多 20 亿个元素是不够的，但 40 亿个元素就足够了。

  1. 我知道大多数“64 位”CPU 实际上有 48 位或 52 位地址。 为了简单起见，我们只使用“64 位地址”。 ↩︎
  2. 溢出检查是一个例外。 设置溢出 UB 意味着编译器可以消除溢出检查，从而有可能提高性能。 然而，我认为这是 C 优先考虑性能而不是正确性的又一个例子，并且这些检查不应该首先被删除。 ↩︎
